coredata.dc:title: Study of ionic water/graphene nanofluids in solar panels under the effects of thermal radiation and slip conditions using experimental data
coredata.prism:publicationName: International Communications in Heat and Mass Transfer
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Yusuf, Abdulhakeem
Bhatti, M.M.
Ellahi, R.
coredata.dc:description:
The combination of the extensive surface area and thermal conductivity of graphene oxide (GO) with the thermal stability and low volatility of ionic liquid (IL) enhances thermal energy efficiency in solar collectors. This study theoretically examined the flow characteristics of various nanofluids in a solar collector, specifically ionic liquid/GO, a mixture of 75% ionic liquid and 25% water (H
2
O)/GO, a 50% ionic liquid and 50% water/GO blend, and water/GO. To accurately assess the effects of buoyancy, solar radiation, nanoparticle absorption, and the overall thermal energy transfer from the boundary to the collector, this study incorporates nonlinear free convection, nonlinear thermal radiation, heat absorption, and slip and jump phenomena under convective conditions. The mathematical model was developed using non-similarity variables, and the results were obtained using the Garlakin weighted residual method (GWRM). This study demonstrates that an increase in the nonlinear free convective parameter, nonlinear thermal radiation, and heat absorption enhances the thermal energy of the nanofluid within the collector. The pure ionic liquid combined with graphene oxide (IL/GO) nanofluid demonstrated a thermal energy efficiency that exceeded that of water by 36.8%, which quantitatively corroborated the experimental result of 37.4%. This indicates that increased IL/GO nanofluid concentrations are optimal for high-intensity solar applications, whereas water is preferable for moderate solar conditions to prevent overheating.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene oxide (GO)
Ionic nanofluid (INF)
Nonlinear thermal radiation
Nonlinear free convection
Convective boundary condition
Solar panel

head.ce:title: Study of ionic water/graphene nanofluids in solar panels under the effects of thermal radiation and slip conditions using experimental data

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The combination of the extensive surface area and thermal conductivity of graphene oxide (GO) with the thermal stability and low volatility of ionic liquid (IL) enhances thermal energy efficiency in solar collectors. This study theoretically examined the flow characteristics of various nanofluids in a solar collector, specifically ionic liquid/GO, a mixture of 75% ionic liquid and 25% water (H
2
O)/GO, a 50% ionic liquid and 50% water/GO blend, and water/GO. To accurately assess the effects of buoyancy, solar radiation, nanoparticle absorption, and the overall thermal energy transfer from the boundary to the collector, this study incorporates nonlinear free convection, nonlinear thermal radiation, heat absorption, and slip and jump phenomena under convective conditions. The mathematical model was developed using non-similarity variables, and the results were obtained using the Garlakin weighted residual method (GWRM). This study demonstrates that an increase in the nonlinear free convective parameter, nonlinear thermal radiation, and heat absorption enhances the thermal energy of the nanofluid within the collector. The pure ionic liquid combined with graphene oxide (IL/GO) nanofluid demonstrated a thermal energy efficiency that exceeded that of water by 36.8%, which quantitatively corroborated the experimental result of 37.4%. This indicates that increased IL/GO nanofluid concentrations are optimal for high-intensity solar applications, whereas water is preferable for moderate solar conditions to prevent overheating.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Climate change refers to the alteration in climate patterns primarily caused by an increase in greenhouse gas emissions. The main factor contributing to global warming is the increase in atmospheric heat retention, caused by the alteration of natural concentration levels due to this excess [1]. Renewable energy sources are advocated as alternatives to fossil fuels to mitigate this impact. The investigation and active participation in the adoption of recent technologies are increasingly important due to the growing energy demands and rising energy prices in the current context.

Solar energy has become the primary alternative energy source to address the increasing global energy demand, as sunlight is the most plentiful and accessible energy resource on the planet. It presents a viable approach to mitigating global warming and attaining sustainable development [2]. Photovoltaic (PV) modules are designed for converting solar energy into electrical energy efficiently. The conversion efficiency of photovoltaic panels for solar energy to electricity is adversely affected, with over 80% of the absorbed solar radiation being either reflected or converted into heat, leading to a rise in the temperature of the panels. The photosolar-thermal (PVT) hybrid solar collector represents a more efficient solution for improving thermal capacitance. Several scientists are currently assessing the feasibility of extracting excess heat generated by a working fluid, including air and water, while also exploring its potential applications. The effectiveness of the integrated solar energy system is mainly influenced by the thermo-optical characteristics of the working fluid and its functionality in practical applications. Common working fluids employed in solar energy systems comprise water, ethylene glycol, Therminol VP-1, and molten salts. These working fluids exhibit inherent limitations. Their application across diverse working conditions is limited by factors such as high vapor pressure, thermal instability at elevated temperatures, inadequate heat transfer properties, elevated melting points, and additional challenges. Water vaporizes at 100° and exhibits a high vapor pressure, rendering it impractical at elevated temperatures. The melting point of molten salts is elevated; however, ethylene glycol and Therminol demonstrate inadequate heat transfer characteristics [3]. Flat plate solar collectors (FPSC), while typically operating at
100
° C
, can achieve this temperature under extreme weather conditions or malfunctions. To prevent overheating, which is a significant concern when using water, it is essential to utilize working fluids with low vapor pressure [4]. It is crucial to develop new working fluids for applications that operate at medium to high temperatures.

The development of new heat transfer fluids using ionic liquids (ILs) is facilitated by their distinctive properties, which allow for the modification of the cation–anion structure to meet specific application requirements and desired physicochemical characteristics. Studies on the thermophysical characteristics of ionic liquids (ILs) indicate that they possess high heat capacity and density, superior chemical and thermal stability, and low vapor pressure. In medium-to-high-temperature heat transfer systems, ionic liquids are considered viable candidates for heat transfer fluid applications due to their advantageous thermophysical properties [5]. The utilization of ionic liquids in solar energy technologies, including solar cells, solar collectors, solar troughs, and solar photovoltaic-thermal panels, has received significant attention recently. The examination of ionic liquids as potential solar thermal fluids is currently underway. The combination of ionic liquids and nanomaterials shows considerable promise as heat transfer fluids, effectively improving thermal properties [6].

Graphene oxide (GO) has been utilized as a nanoparticle in numerous base fluids owing to its extensive surface area, exceptional thermal conductivity, and stability. Diffused 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium graphene oxide was investigated by Mozaffarian et al. [11], who discovered that 2 weight percent GO nanoparticles increased thermal conductivity and specific heat capacity by 6.5% and 42%, respectively. By 7.2% weight percent GO nanoparticles, the heat transfer coefficient increased by 0.5. The viscosity of graphene oxide/water nanofluids was investigated by Cabaleiro et al. [12], who reported an increase of up to 130% for concentrations between 0.0005% and 0.1%.

The flow characteristics, heat transfer, viscosity, and thermal conductivity of a GO-CuO water-based nanofluid were examined by Ugwekar et al. [13]. Consistent with standard fluid dynamics, the study revealed that viscosity increased with higher concentrations of GO-CuO and decreased with rising thermal energy. Across various concentrations of GO-CuO, the findings similarly indicated an increased coefficient of heat transfer associated with a higher Reynolds number. In their investigation, Ranjbarzadeh et al. [14] found that a graphene oxide-silicon oxide nanofluid based on water exhibited a viscosity increase of 345% compared to the base fluid. Miao et al. [15] investigated heat transmission and friction reduction in graphene oxide/water nanofluids, noting a 71% reduction in the friction coefficient at 0.1 vol % and an increase in the Nusselt number ratio from 1.02 to 1.18 with higher concentrations of graphene oxide.

A variety of scientists have employed the equations of fluid momentum, temperature, and concentration to conduct theoretical investigations into the effects of the thermal energy of nanofluids. Shafiq et al. [16] demonstrated the significance of convective boundary conditions and thermal slip in the context of three-dimensional rotating Darcy-Forchheimer nanofluid flow. Krishna and Kumar [17] specifics the non-linear heat radiation, chemical reaction, and slip effects concerning unsteady MHD Jeffreys nanofluid flow over a stretching sheet. The study conducted by Sajid et al. [18] examines the consequences of catalytic reactions on the flow of 3D tetra hybrid nanofluids through oil rig solar panel sheets, utilizing an oil extraction case study as a basis for analysis. To investigate the flow characteristics over a Riga plate, Yusuf et al. [19] employed a ternary nanofluid composed of water with suspended silver, copper, and titanium dioxide nanoparticles. The study included Thomson and Troian slip conditions, variable viscosity, and nonlinear mixed convection. Moulefera et al. [20] have recently demonstrated the innovative combination of Graphene oxide (GO) nanoparticles and 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium acetate ionic liquid (IL) in an experimental flat plate solar thermal collector (FPSC), resulting in the formation of novel ionanofluids (INF) for the first time. The results indicated a 37.4% enhancement in efficiency. Further investigation on this topic can be found in Refs. [21–26].

The available literature indicates the theoretical investigation of nonlinear free convection, nonlinear thermal radiation, heat absorption, temperature jump, and velocity slip with a convective boundary regarding the flow of IL/GO in solar energy has not been addressed. To rectify these deficiencies, we investigate the flow of IL/GO, IL(75%)-H
2
O(25%)/GO, IL(50%)-H
2
O(50%)/GO, and H
2
O/GO inside a solar panel. The unique characteristics of graphene oxide (GO), noted for its extensive surface area, together with the thermal and chemical stability of the ionic liquid (IL), enable the use of the IL/GO nanofluid in various thermal engineering applications. These applications include heat transfer fluids, thermal interface materials, phase change materials, adjustable thermal insulators, electronic cooling systems, thermally conductive coatings, and thermal energy storage technologies. This work utilizes nonlinear free convection, thermal radiation, slip, leap, and convective barriers to precisely characterize the intricate behavior of ionic nanofluids (INFs) in thermal systems. This integration enables a more precise representation of the thermal processes in flat solar panel collectors. Sunlight functions as the principal energy source for solar panels, requiring the evaluation of nonlinear thermal radiation. A nonlinear free convective component is included owing to the nonlinear temperature gradient caused by heat absorption. Ultimately, to diminish flow resistance at the panel border and to consider the predominant thermal energy transmitted to the collector or storage, the Lee velocity term, in conjunction with temperature jump and convective boundary conditions, is included.

Considered is a two-dimensional flow of a nanofluid that comprises graphen oxide (GO) nanoparticles, ionic liquid (IL), and water (H
2
O) as base fluids. The inclusion of GO in the mixture, known for its superior heat absorption capacity from primary energy sources such as sunlight, enhances the fluid’s effectiveness as a heat absorber. The heat absorption is non-uniform, with a concentration near the absorber plate. This phenomenon creates a nonlinear temperature gradient within the nanofluid, which triggers nonlinear free convection. The surface fluid velocity is expressed as
u
w
( x )
= a x
, with the fluid temperature denoted as
T
f
and the ambient temperature represented as
T
∞
. Viscous dissipation is disregarded due to the low momentum of the flow field. Joule heating is disregarded as the main energy input is solar radiation.

The surface temperature pertains to the temperature of the solar panel in contact with the fluid. The thermal gradient between the panel and the INFs governs heat transfer. The solar panel absorbs solar radiation, resulting in a boost in surface temperature. Heat is subsequently transferred to the INFs through convective heat transfer.

To reduce fluid resistance at the boundary (velocity slip), it is essential to examine the impact of the temperature gradient between the graphene oxide (GO) and the base fluids (ionic liquid and water), which induces relative motion at the boundary (Lee thermal slip). Additionally, the heat transfer at the interface between the nanofluid and the solar panel surface (convective boundary) must be described. The flat plate geometry’s physical relevance lies in its provision of a uniform heat transfer surface, often used in laminar flow analysis. Its simplicity in fabrication facilitates uniform solar energy absorption and improved heat transmission. Consequently, this analysis is performed within the rectangular coordinate system ‘
x y
’ as seen in Fig. 1, with the model formulations expressed as [27–29]:

Eq. (10) and the nonlinear dimensionless partial differential Eqs. (8)–(9) are solved using the Galerkin weighted residual method (GWRM). Due to the Flexibility of GWRM in handling complex geometry such as the differential equations of the following type, GWRM attempts to find an approximation:
(14)
L
1
[ χ
( x , y )
]
+ g
( x , y )
= 0 ,
in a given domain D ,
where
L
1
indicates the partial differential operator,
χ
( x , y )
indicates the unknown dependent function, and
g
( x , y )
indicates the independent function in the given domain D .

.
The solution in Eq. (15) is approximated to ensure compliance with the boundary condition. Substituting Eq. (15) into Eq. (14) yielded a residual function
R
( x )
. Minimizing
R
( x )
inside the specified domain D and computing the integral of the product of weight functions
χ
j
( x )
with residue
R
( x , y )
across the full domain D to provide zero for
i ≥ 0 , n
and
j ≥ 0 , m
. Namely,
(16)

Considering the boundary condition ranges from zero to infinity, each equation in Eq. (16) is integrated with the Gauss–Laguerre formula, resulting in a system of equations. The subsequent algebraic equations must be resolved to determine the values of
a
i , j
.

The Gauss–Laguerre formula is used in the following form as [32]:
(17)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the GWRM to the present problem
ce:section.ce:para:
For
F
( ξ , η )
and
Θ
( ξ , η )
, the subsequent trial solutions are proposed utilizing the Galerkin weighted residual approach:
(20)
F
( ξ , η )
= 1 −

Substituting Eq. (20) into the boundary condition of the ODE in Eq. (10) with the selection of
m = 16
yields:

Eq. (10) promptly fulfills the boundary conditions at infinity. By substituting Eq. (20) into Eqs. (8) and (9), the residual functions
R
F
(
a
i , j
,

, ξ , η )
and
R
Θ
(

i , j
,

, ξ , η )
for
i ,
j = 0 , 1 , 2 , … , 16
were derived. Initially, we choose the integral of the product of the weight function
e
−
j η
3
and the residual function to minimize the residual errors.
(24)
∫
0
∞
R
F
e
−
i ξ + j η
3
d η = 0 ,
∫
0
∞

−

3
d η = 0 ,
Employing the aforementioned equation in conjunction with Eqs. (21) to (23) yields
3 m + 1
nonlinear algebraic equations with
3 m + 1
unknown coefficients (
a
i , j
,
b
i , j
). The constants
a
i , j
and
b
i , j
are determined by solving these algebraic equations at
ξ = 1
using the computer-assisted symbolic program MATHEMATICA.

This section is focused on analyzing the numerical outcomes of various relevant parameters. Specifically, the mechanism of ionic nanofluids with water nanofluid (IL/GO, IL-H
2
O(75%–25%)/GO, IL-H
2
O(50%–50%)/GO, H
2
O/GO) has been discussed. In the laboratory experiment, the volume fraction of GO is maintained constant throughout the study. The thermophysical quantities derived from the experimental data are presented in Table 3. The current problem is addressed through the GWRM, demonstrating a strong correlation with existing literature demonstrated in Table 4 for various Prandtl number values. This validates the current results and confirms the accuracy of the Galerkin weighted residual approach. The selected parametric values for the graphical representation are as follows:Pr
= 1 − 6 . 13 ,
λ
1
=
λ
2
= 10 . 0
,
β
1
=
β
2
= 0 . 5
,
β
t
= 0 . 7
,
N
r
= 0 . 5
.

The heat absorption parameter
Q
0
denotes the rate at which the fluid assimilates thermal energy from sunlight. The temperature drop shown in Fig. 2(b) indicates that the augmented heat absorption parameter facilitates effective energy redistribution instead of inducing heat stagnation in the fluids. This is especially beneficial for mitigating thermal strains in the collector components (see [38]).

The nonlinear radiation parameter
N
r
signifies the impact of radiative heat transfer characterized by a nonlinear correlation. As the radiation parameter escalates, the nanofluids absorb more thermal energy from sunlight, resulting in an increase in temperature, as seen in Fig. 2(c). This signifies that the nanofluids are proficiently absorbing and retaining radiative energy, hence augmenting their function as efficient heat transfer mediums in the solar collector. A similar scenario is delineated in [39].

An elevated Biot number
β
t
indicates enhanced convective heat transfer between the nanofluids and the absorber plate in the solar collector. This effective heat transfer mechanism guarantees that the fluid receives more thermal energy from the barrier, elevating its temperature in Fig. 2(d). The elevation in temperature with an increased
β
t
indicates that the fluids are efficiently transferring the heat absorbed at the boundary into their bulk, resulting in a uniform temperature increase. The incorporation of graphene oxide (GO) augments the thermal conductivity of the fluids, facilitating an equitable heat distribution within the fluid, even at elevated
β
t
levels.

Fig. 3(a–f) illustrate the variation of the Nusselt number (
− N u
) with regard to
β
t
,
λ
1
,
Q
0
,
N
r
,
β
1
,
β
2
correspondingly. A negative Nusselt number (
− N u
) signifies that the nanofluids are effectively dissipating the thermal energy acquired from solar radiation to the collector system. The increase in the Nusselt number resulting from the enhancement of
β
t
in Fig. 3(a) signifies an improvement in the capacity of the nanofluids to transmit absorbed solar energy to the absorber plate more efficiently. This guarantees that thermal energy is promptly accessible for further operations, including storage or conversion into electricity. Given that buoyancy is significant, the elevated free convective parameter facilitates more efficient thermal energy transfer independent of external pumping devices.

The enhanced convection resulting from buoyancy sustains a robust thermal gradient, facilitating continuous heat transfer from the nanofluid to the collector surface, thereby elevating the Nusselt number in Fig. 3(b). The enhanced heat absorption and transfer to the collector surface, as seen by the rise in the Nusselt number in Fig. 3(c), enhances the system’s efficiency in converting solar radiation into thermal energy. The fluid’s ability to absorb and effectively transmit energy improves the overall thermal efficiency of the solar collector. This is especially important for systems aimed at optimizing energy collection and storage, since it enhances heat use and reduces thermal losses.

The radiation parameter is shown to reduce the Nusselt number in Fig. 3(d). This indicates that radiation is increasingly dominating the heat transfer process, with a greater proportion of the energy received by the nanofluids being expelled as radiation. The presence of velocity slip (
β
1
) in nanofluids reduces friction between the fluids and the collector surface, resulting in a diminished boundary layer.

A diminished Nusselt number resulting from heightened velocity slip in Fig. 3(e) indicates a decreased transfer of thermal energy from the nanofluids to the collection plate. This may reduce the efficiency of energy conversion from solar radiation to thermal energy in the collector. As a result, the system may fail to achieve the intended operating temperature or may need more time to heat, thus impacting the thermal storage and overall efficiency of the solar collector.

The elevation of the Nusselt number resulting from the temperature gradient in Fig. 3(f) indicates that the interaction between the nanofluids and the collector surface enhances heat transfer efficiency. The leap facilitates enhanced thermal energy transfer, enabling the collector to assimilate more heat from the nanofluid. The temperature increase enhances heat transmission, hence improving the system’s efficiency in transmitting absorbed solar energy to the collector surface and reducing thermal energy loss inside the nanofluid.

Fig. 4(a–d) illustrate the streamline flows for IL/GO, IL-H
2
O(75%–25%)/GO, IL-H
2
O(50%–50%)/GO ionic nanofluids, and H
2
O/GO water nanofluid, respectively. The center area
( − 0 . 5 – 0 . 5 )
of the flow is expected to undergo increased flow acceleration owing to the collector’s design. This acceleration results in a reduced velocity boundary layer and amplifies shear stress, causing streamlines to become densely packed. The broader spacing of streamlines, particularly at the peripheries, signifies less fluid velocity, hence impairing the efficacy of convective heat transfer in these areas. The GO particles may agglomerate under certain circumstances, affecting flow continuity and thereby breaking streamlines in some areas. The absorber plate in solar collectors may induce uneven heating, resulting in discrepancies in thermal expansion and density, which exhibit as anomalies in streamline patterns.

Fig. 5(a–d) illustrate the streamline density map for IL/GO, IL-H
2
O(75%–25%)/GO, IL-H
2
O(50%–50%)/GO ionic nanofluids and H
2
O/GO aqueous nanofluid, respectively. Solar collectors often exhibit spatially uneven heating. Consequently, when thermal conductivity and heat capacity vary with the addition of water, the heat absorption pattern changes, influencing streamline behavior. Consequently, the streamlines continue to increase with further disruptions, as seen in Fig. 5(a–d). Incorporating water into ionic liquids often decreases the viscosity of the blend. A reduced viscosity facilitates smoother flow and enhances heat transfer efficiency within the fluid. As viscosity diminishes, the fluid may more effectively adapt to the heat source and transmit energy with greater efficiency. This is evident in the rising values of the contour streamlines seen in Fig. 6(a–d).

The basic objective of solar collectors is to optimize the absorption and even distribution of solar thermal energy. The alignment of contour isotherm lines with the addition of water in the figures. Fig. 7(a–d) indicates a more uniform temperature distribution, which mitigates thermal loads on materials, hence enhancing system durability.

The present study examined the theoretical analysis of nonlinear free convective flow of three ionic nanofluids and water (IL/GO, IL-H
2
O(75%–25%)/GO, IL-H
2
O(50%–50%)/GO, H
2
O/GO) including nonlinear radiation. The research incorporates heat absorption, velocity slip with temperature jump, and convective boundary conditions. Experimental data including dynamic viscosity, density, specific heat capacity, and thermal conductivity of the ionic liquid blended with graphene oxide and water at 20° is used to precisely formulate the mathematical model. The Garlakin weighted residual techniques are used to provide accurate and efficient solutions for the transformed equations of the given problem, allowing for flexibility in the implementation of boundary conditions. This ongoing research demonstrates that:

ce:list-item.ce:label: i.
ce:list-item.ce:para: Improved thermal stability of the nanofluids to transmit thermal energy to the solar collector is the result of a surge in nonlinear free convection.

ce:list-item.ce:label: ii.
ce:list-item.ce:para: A raise in the heat absorption parameter mitigates thermal stress in the collector.

ce:list-item.ce:label: iii.
ce:list-item.ce:para: Nanofluids exhibit enhanced thermal energy absorption attributed to an improved nonlinear radiation parameter.

ce:list-item.ce:label: iv.
ce:list-item.ce:para: The observed temperature escalation due to an accelerated Biot number indicates that the fluids efficiently transfer the heat absorbed at the boundary to their bulk.

ce:list-item.ce:label: v.
ce:list-item.ce:para: The Nusselt number increases with an accelerated Biot number, a nonlinear free convection heat absorption parameter, and a temperature jump. The inverse scenario involves nonlinear radiation and velocity slip.

ce:list-item.ce:label: vi.
ce:list-item.ce:para:
The pure ionic liquid combined with graphene oxide (IL/GO) nanofluid demonstrated superior thermal energy efficiency compared to IL-H
2
O(75%–25%)/GO, IL-H
2
O(50%–50%)/GO, and H
2
O/GO, achieving performance improvements of 9.001%, 36.8%, and 72.8% respectively, thereby validating the experimental results.

ce:list-item.ce:label: vii.
ce:list-item.ce:para: The IL/GO nanofluid exhibited a lower temperature compared to the other parameters across all conditions. Indicate its stability and thermal management in comparison to other possibilities in solar energy applications.

ce:section.ce:para:
Compared to water-based systems, ionic liquid/ graphene oxide nanofluids have high thermal conductivity and heat capacity. Using a higher ionic liquid concentration (75% IL
+ 25% water/GO ionic liquid) exhibits better heat absorption and retention ability, leading these fluids to be more suitable for high-intensity solar usage. In contrast, a lower ionic liquid loading (50% IL + 50% water/GO ionic liquid) or water-based (water/GO) nanofluids, are more suitable for moderate solar conditions, where overheating prevention is crucial. The fluid recipe can also be tailoredbased on collector configuration, operating temperatures and environmental conditions. Though the analysis performed in this study is limited to two-dimensional steady-state study, it would warrant further analysis on three-dimensional and unsteady-state conditions byintroducing various further parameters to yield better results. In addition, the present study did not explore the effectsof non-Newtonian fluids; this may be explored in future studies.

Abdulhakeem Yusuf: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. M.M. Bhatti: Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration. R. Ellahi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
